questions.question: Why is CDN invalidation often treated as a control-plane problem rather than only a cache-node problem?
options: Because the difficult part is safely distributing purge intent, sequencing updates, and explaining propagation globally.
Because CDN caches never store metadata about objects.
Because TTLs make purge requests unnecessary in production.
Because POPs cannot receive any asynchronous updates.
questions.explanation: Global purge behavior depends on coordination, rollout, and fanout, not just local eviction.

questions.question: What usually makes tag-based purges harder than single-object purges?
options: They require more metadata, more fanout pressure, and tighter cardinality controls.
They only work when browser caches are disabled.
They guarantee stronger consistency automatically.
They eliminate the need for cache keys.
questions.explanation: Tag purges are powerful, but they add metadata indexing and propagation cost.

questions.question: Why is versioned invalidation often preferred over scanning caches for matching objects?
options: It makes global invalidation cheaper and safer by letting old entries age out while new versions stop serving.
It forces every purge to bypass the control plane entirely.
It guarantees browser caches refresh instantly.
It removes the need for observability around stale hits.
questions.explanation: Versioning avoids expensive global delete walks and gives cleaner semantics under scale.

questions.question: Which metric best detects that purge propagation is lagging even though the purge API is healthy?
options: POP apply lag and stale-hit rate after purge.
Only global CPU utilization on origin servers.
The number of architecture reviews completed last quarter.
TLS certificate renewal count.
questions.explanation: Propagation lag becomes visible in POP-level apply delay and stale-serving after purge.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of supporting full-zone purges generously?
options: Better customer simplicity, but much higher blast radius and control-plane cost.
Lower metadata use with no operational downside.
Perfect freshness with lower origin traffic automatically.
No need for per-tenant quotas anymore.
questions.explanation: Large-scope purges are easy for customers but expensive and risky for the platform.

questions.question: Why should purge requests be rate-limited or quota-controlled per tenant?
options: To prevent one tenant from turning purge fanout into a global control-plane incident.
To ensure stale content is always served longer.
To make object purges impossible for free plans.
To avoid storing request IDs.
questions.explanation: Per-tenant controls keep purge load from becoming a fleet-wide reliability problem.

questions.question: If some POPs are partitioned from the purge stream, what is the strongest design need?
options: Replayable, idempotent purge delivery with visibility into which POPs are behind.
Immediate deletion of the origin data store.
Disabling all caching globally until the partition clears.
Relying on operator memory instead of tracking lag.
questions.explanation: Partitions happen, so purge delivery needs replay and explicit lag tracking.

questions.question: What hidden product risk appears when customers say a purge 'failed' even though CDN nodes applied it?
options: Another cache layer such as browsers or intermediaries may still be serving stale content, so semantics must be explained clearly.
Purge systems cannot work with authenticated requests.
The cache key is always irrelevant to the result.
The only remaining cause is TLS handshake latency.
questions.explanation: A purge may succeed at one layer while users still see stale content from another cache.
